1: Ps 82: The Divine Council Psalm — the Most Theologically Significant Asaph Psalm. Ps 82 is one of the most theologically charged and debated texts in the OT. It depicts YHWH presiding over the adat El (assembly/council of El/God) — the divine council of the ANE, which in Ugaritic texts is El's court of divine beings. The three main interpretations: (1) YHWH judging the pagan gods of the nations (the ANE polytheistic framework being absorbed and transcended); (2) YHWH judging corrupt human judges who have been called 'gods' by virtue of their divine-representative role (cf. Exod 21:6; 22:8-9, 28; John 10:34-35); (3) YHWH judging angelic beings entrusted with governance of the nations (Deut 32:8: 'according to the number of the sons of God'; cf. Dan 10). Jesus quotes v6 in John 10:34-35 in the context of the charge that claiming divine sonship is blasphemy.
2: 'How long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked?' — the divine indictment of the council members. Whether divine beings or human judges, the charge is the same: unjust judgment and partiality toward the wicked. In the ANE, the divine council was supposed to ensure cosmic justice; corrupt council-members meant a cosmos in moral disorder. The failure of justice in the created order is traced to corruption at the governing level.
3: 'Give justice to the weak and the fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute' — the divine council's mandate: to protect the vulnerable. In ancient Israel's legal system, the 'elders at the gate' were responsible for legal protection of the widow, orphan, and sojourner (Exod 22:22-24; Deut 10:18; 27:19). The divine council's members share this mandate at the cosmic level. Their failure to enforce it represents a catastrophic failure of the created moral order.
4: 'Rescue the weak and the needy; deliver them from the hand of the wicked' — the rescue mandate: not merely legal adjudication but active deliverance. The weak (dal), needy (evyon), and those in the hand of the wicked (rasha) are the triple-protected class of covenant law. YHWH's own self-description includes protection of these groups (Deut 10:17-18: 'the LORD your God... executes justice for the fatherless and the widow, and loves the sojourner'); the council-members are supposed to extend this protection throughout creation.
5: 'They have neither knowledge nor understanding, they walk about in darkness; all the foundations of the earth are shaken' — the diagnosis of the council's failure: lo yadu velo yavinnu (they do not know and they do not understand) — moral and intellectual blindness. 'Walk about in darkness' (bachashechah yithallachu) — the darkness of moral disorder. 'All the foundations of the earth are shaken' (yimotu kol-mosdei aretz) — the cosmic consequences of judicial corruption: when those who govern the creation fail, the physical creation itself becomes unstable. Moral disorder has cosmological effects.
6: 'I said, You are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you' — ani amarti elohim atem uvene elyon kulchem — the divine declaration of the council-members' status: they are elohim (gods/divine beings) and bene elyon (sons of the Most High). This was their appointed dignity. Jesus quotes this verse in John 10:34-35 to argue: if the OT calls those to whom the word of God came 'gods,' then claiming to be the Son of God is not blasphemy. The verse establishes a precedent for divine-representative status through divine appointment.
7: 'Nevertheless, like men you shall die, and fall like any prince' — the reversal of the dignity of v6: despite being called elohim, they will die ke'adam (like men/Adam). The mortality of the divine council members is the judgment for their moral failure. In the ANE, death was what distinguished mortals from the gods; here the gods are condemned to mortal death. 'Fall like any prince' (kachad hasarim tipolu) — their end is the same as the most powerful human ruler who has been deposed: they fall.
8: 'Arise, O God, judge the earth; for you shall inherit all the nations!' — the psalm closes with a prayer for YHWH to take direct sovereignty over all the nations, bypassing the corrupt council. 'Arise, O God' (qumah Elohim) — the divine arising to action. 'Judge the earth' (shophta haaretz) — YHWH himself exercising the justice that the council members failed to render. 'You shall inherit all the nations' (ki attah tinchal bekhol hagoyim) — the eschatological universalism: YHWH's inheritance is all peoples, not just Israel. This prayer forms the basis of the NT's universal mission and the eschatological judgment of all nations.
